w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Bolti Nadi (Save River – Clean River Campaign)

In 2019, the Bolti Nadi initiative undertook a comprehensive 90-kilometer on-foot expedition along the Sakri River in Chhattisgarh, traversing from its origin in Kariyama Village in Kawardha district to its confluence with the Half River in Darhi Village in Bemetara district over four continuous days. The journey was designed as a multi-dimensional research mission combining hydrological assessment, ecological documentation, and community consultation. The team collected water samples from villages along the riverbanks, documenting quality parameters for drinking and agricultural use, while assessing the river's influence on local agriculture, livestock, and the surrounding ecosystem across its entire course. Historical and cultural documentation was also undertaken, including visits to heritage sites such as the 14th century

Temple, recognizing rivers as repositories of civilization. Direct consultations were held with community members in every village, revealing diverse location-specific challenges. Critical observations included the drastic decline in water flow downstream from the origin, anicuts with questionable effectiveness where downstream areas remained dry, borewells drilled up to 1000 feet with diminishing returns, the erosion of traditional river culture, and severe bank erosion at
and Kodar village where the river had widened threatening agricultural lands. Ongoing government initiatives under the Narva, Garva,
, and Bari scheme were documented, including fodder cultivation for Gothans and silt removal from canals through MGNREGA

. Upon completion, a comprehensive research report with actionable recommendations was submitted to the district administrations of Kawardha and Bemetara and other concerned departments. The findings contributed to policy discourse, leading to an integrated rejuvenation program for the Sakri River involving multi-departmental coordination. This included planting commercial and medicinal trees along riverbanks under the Mukhyamantri Vriksh Sampada Yojana, constructing check dams and gabions at erosion-prone sites to prevent further bank cutting, and desilting structures to ensure water availability across 59 villages. A sewage treatment plant worth approximately two crore rupees was established by the Kawardha municipal corporation to address river pollution. MGNREGA

teams carried out cleaning and desilting works across almost the entire river belt, significantly improving water flow. The journey was documented through film, screened at educational institutions across Chhattisgarh and submitted to film festivals in over 50 countries. The Sakri River Walk demonstrated that systematic grassroots research combining scientific data, community consultation, and cultural documentation, when shared with relevant authorities, can inform evidence-based policy and
concrete action for river rejuvenation through civil society and government collaboration.
